one_line_takeaway: Benign developer phrasing and small typos reliably push production LLMs to import non-existent or misspelled packages -- up to 99% of tasks for fully fabricated names -- turning ordinary coding prompts into a concrete slopsquatting/typosquatting supply-chain attack surface, quantified and packaged as the LIBHALLUBENCH benchmark.
key_finding: LLMs are strikingly non-robust to natural prompt variation for library names. Adjective descriptions are essentially ignored (~0% name hallucination), but year-based phrasing 'from 2025' induces name hallucinations in up to 84.74% of tasks (GPT-4o-mini). User mistakes are worse: a single-character misspelling makes GPT-5-mini use the wrong library in 25.86% of tasks, multi-character typos reach 79.44% (GPT-5-mini THR), and fully fabricated library names are accepted in up to 99.22% of tasks (GPT-5-mini) -- with GPT-4o-mini (92.52%) and Ministral-8B (90.50%) also above 90%. Rather than correcting an obvious typo of a popular package (e.g. panfas->pandas, nimpy->numpy), models comply, consistent with sycophancy. Library MEMBER hallucinations are far milder and scale less clearly with error severity (fake members ~31% avg task usage vs ~69% for fake names). Qwen-2.5-Coder is the most robust to fake names (20.72% THR). Mitigations are partial: prompt engineering is inconsistent (CoT/step-back sometimes worsen it); a PyPI-existence tool sharply cuts but does not eliminate hallucinations because the model must still decide when to call it.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first systematic study of how realistic, user-level prompt variations trigger library-related hallucinations in LLM-generated code. It distinguishes two verifiable failure classes -- library NAME hallucinations (importing a non-existent package) and library MEMBER hallucinations (calling a non-existent function/class from a valid package) -- and measures both across seven production LLMs. Its own novel contributions (as opposed to the RAG/two-stage mitigation lines it surveys in Related Work) are: (1) quantifying the effect of developer-inspired language (adjectives, year-based phrasing) and controlled user mistakes (1-char / multi-char misspellings, fully fabricated names); (2) uncovering a link between minor prompt errors and typosquatting/slopsquatting supply-chain risk; and (3) LIBHALLUBENCH, a reproducible benchmark of 4,173 labelled prompts derived from 321 seed tasks, plus a released Python evaluation library.
threat_model: Supply-chain attacks via LLM code assistants. Two attacker-relevant mechanisms: slopsquatting (an attacker registers a package name that LLMs frequently hallucinate, so hallucinated imports resolve to attacker-controlled code) and typosquatting amplification (developers make a small typo of a real library, and instead of correcting it the LLM confidently uses the misspelled/fabricated name, propagating the malicious lookalike). The trigger surface is ordinary, non-adversarial developer prompting: asking for a library 'from 2025', requesting 'lesser known'/'hidden gem' packages, or including an accidental one-character typo. The paper's threat framing is grounded in the paper's own experiments, not invented -- it shows these benign phrasings reliably produce invalid imports that a package manager would install by default (latest version) with no version pin.
limitations: Authors' stated limits: generalisability is bounded to a finite set of tasks, ecosystems and model versions (though CodeInsight adds JavaScript/Rust and hundreds more libraries). Internal-validity threats in automated extraction and prompt construction, mitigated by unit tests, manual validation on 200 responses (precision/recall in appendix), and ablations over decoding and error-generation. External-validity threats from dataset bias (mitigated by excluding ground-truth-revealing tasks; residual leakage would only reduce hallucinations, making results conservative) and LLM nondeterminism/opaque updates (mitigated by version-pinning models and repeating runs). Reviewer-added: the study is descriptive/measurement-focused -- it demonstrates end-to-end exploitation risk only indirectly (no live malicious-package experiment), member-hallucination detection depends on doc-scraping coverage, and closed models are evaluated at fixed API defaults so results may drift with silent model updates.

datasets_used: BigCodeBench (seed): 1,140 Python tasks, 7 domains; filtered to 356 tasks / 30 distinct libraries (10% preliminary, 90% main; 3 samples/prompt)
Software Recommendations StackExchange (SRSE): >20,000 questions filtered to 3,917 library-related, clustered into 9 adjective descriptors plus year-based prompts
CodeInsight (generalisation): 500 tasks rewritten language-agnostic; ground-truth libraries -> 140 Python, 161 JavaScript, 184 Rust distinct libraries
LIBHALLUBENCH (released): 4,173 labelled prompts derived from 321 seed tasks (year-, rarity-, and error-based conditions)
PyPI as name oracle; scraped latest-version official library documentation as member oracle
quantitative_results: Fake library name accepted (THR): GPT-5-mini 99.22%, GPT-4o-mini 92.52%, Ministral-8B 90.50%, Claude-4.5-Haiku 76.95%, DeepSeek-V3.1 57.79%, Llama-3.3 47.04%, Qwen-2.5-Coder 20.72%
One-character misspelling, library name (THR): GPT-5-mini 25.86% (highest); Ministral-8B 4.52%; GPT-4o-mini 4.05%; Qwen-2.5-Coder 0.31%
Multi-character misspelling, library name (THR): GPT-5-mini 79.44%, GPT-4o-mini 34.58%, DeepSeek-V3.1 27.41%, Ministral-8B 25.39%

Year 'from 2025' library-name hallucination (THR): GPT-4o-mini 84.74%, GPT-5-mini 63.86%, Qwen-2.5-Coder 61.68%, DeepSeek-V3.1 38.01%, Ministral-8B 32.40%; Llama-3.3 1.87% and Claude-4.5-Haiku 0.62% most robust
Year progression: GPT-4o-mini +34% responses from 2023->2024; GPT-5-mini +32% from 2024->2025
Adjective-based descriptions: name hallucinations ~0% across all models (all <1%); models default to preferred libraries
Library MEMBER hallucinations mild: no-description RHR ~2-6%; fake member ~31% avg task usage vs ~69% for fake names; GPT-5-mini fake-member THR 76.01% (highest)
Rarity phrasing (avg across models, THR): 'lesser known' 1.92%, 'not widely used' 3.01%, 'hidden gem' 5.35%
Detection validated manually on 200 high-risk responses (precision/recall in appendix)
baselines_compared: Seven LLMs compared against each other: GPT-4o-mini, GPT-5-mini, Ministral-8B, Qwen2.5-Coder, Llama-3.3, DeepSeek-V3.1, Claude-4.5-Haiku (all at API default params, no system prompt, fresh sessions)
Prompt-engineering strategies: chain-of-thought, step-back, self-analysis, explicit library-existence instruction (inconsistent; CoT/step-back sometimes worsen hallucinations)
Tool-use mitigation: lightweight PyPI-existence tool on GPT-4o-mini and GPT-5-mini (large reductions but incomplete)
Cross-ecosystem: Python vs JavaScript vs Rust on CodeInsight-derived tasks
